Details:
Removal of benzene by biological treatment was found to be quite effective. One bench-scale study employing a biomass reactor reported removal of approximately 99 percent by mixed microbial cultures obtained from combined sludge from domestic sewage, the paper industry, and poultry slaughterhouse wastewater. A pilot study performed with a fluidized bed reactor treated contaminated groundwater water with bacteria colonized as a biofilm on GAC also achieved 99 percent removal. Another bench-scale study performed with a strain of Rhodococcus isolated from a chemical spill in Sydney, Australia removed 89 to 95 percent of the initial benzene concentration.
Slightly less effective, a bench-scale study performed with cultures isolated from contaminated gas station soil in Macau, China achieved 83 percent removal of influent benzene. Finally, a benzene removal range varying between 5 and 80 percent was obtained using a mixed microbial culture from chemical wastewater in South Korea.
July 2009
